app.name: E-App
app.tagline: Home energy analysis – easy to do yourself.

errors.invalidEmail: Please enter a valid email address.

info.buildingYear: The year of construction reveals the typical insulation standard and helps estimate the energy demand.
info.windowAge: Older windows often have higher heat losses – the age helps with the assessment.
info.ventilation: HRV stands for heat recovery: the ventilation uses the warmth of the exhaust air to pre-heat fresh air.
info.insulation: A rough self-assessment is enough – you don't need to measure anything precisely.
info.goals: Your goals help the app prioritise the right measurements and tips.
info.occupancy: As an owner, larger measures are often possible than when renting.
info.persons: The number of people significantly affects hot water and electricity use.
info.livingArea: The heated living area is a key figure for the heating demand.
info.heatGenerators: Multiple selection is possible if several systems heat in parallel.
info.hotWater: Is the hot water produced by the heating system or by a separate device?
info.pv: A PV system supplies your own electricity and changes the energy balance.
info.fireplace: An additional fireplace/stove adds room heat and should be recorded.
info.instruments: Select the devices you own – per device you can pick a type and see recommendations.
info.smartHome: Smart devices can provide consumption data and improve the analysis.
info.energyCost: A rough estimate is enough to express savings potential in euros.
info.renovation: Renovations improve the insulation standard and reduce the energy demand.
info.location: Your postal code lets regional climate and weather data flow in later.

report.title: Reports
report.subtitle: Profile, measurements and monitoring as PDF.

measurements.title: Measurements
measurements.description: Results of your checks with rating and savings potential.

monitoring.description: Meter readings, consumption and trends as charts.

total.title: Overall

overview.hint: You set scope and time range in the next step.

page.title: Measurements
page.subtitle: Simple, guided checks for your home.

measurements.allDone: Great – you've completed all available measurements.

byRoom.noRooms: You haven't selected any rooms yet.

showerhead.title: Showerhead Test
showerhead.short: Measure your shower's water flow and uncover savings potential.

intro.videoAlt: Animation: water filling a measuring cup under a showerhead
details: Use a container of known volume – e.g. a measuring cup or bucket with a 5-litre mark.
Turn on the shower the way you normally shower (usual temperature and strength).
Hold the container fully under the stream and start the stopwatch at the same time.
Stop the time exactly when the water reaches the mark.
Tip: measure two or three times and take the average for a more accurate result.
With an instantaneous water heater: let it run briefly until the temperature is stable.

run.litersInfo: The volume of your container. Standard is 5 litres.
run.secondsInfo: The time in seconds until the container was full.

result.sourceNote: Estimated for hot water via {{source}}. More accurate with your price set.

summary.good: Very efficient – your showerhead uses little water.
summary.medium: Medium usage – there's still savings potential.
summary.high: High usage – a water-saving insert pays off.

tip.medium: A water-saving showerhead (~8 L/min) noticeably reduces hot water demand.
tip.high: A water-saving showerhead (~8 L/min) clearly lowers hot water demand – pays off quickly.

fridge.title: Fridge Check
fridge.short: Check the temperature and energy use of your fridge.

intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person measuring the temperature inside the fridge
steps: Put a thermometer in the fridge
details: Place the thermometer in the middle of the fridge – ideally in a glass of water, which shows the real storage temperature.
Close the door and wait several hours (overnight is best) before reading it.
Recommended: 5–7 °C in the fridge and about −18 °C in the freezer compartment.
Every degree colder than necessary noticeably increases electricity use.
While you're at it, check the door seal – if it no longer closes tightly, the appliance cools far more than needed.

run.hint: Rule of thumb: each 1 °C warmer (towards 7 °C) saves ~6% electricity. Turn the dial down and re-measure after a few hours.
run.energyHint: Plug an energy-cost meter between socket and fridge: read the accumulated kWh and runtime (hours) before and after the change.

method.delta: From the achieved temperature difference (~6% per °C).
method.estimate: Potential of setting it to 7 °C (~6% per °C) – not adjusted yet.

status.tooCold: Set too cold – this wastes electricity.

standby.title: Standby Check
standby.short: Track down hidden power drains in standby mode.

intro.needs: You'll need a power meter (energy cost meter).
intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person measuring a TV's standby power
steps: Plug the meter between socket & device
Put device in standby, read the watts
details: For this check you need a simple plug-in power meter (energy cost meter).
Plug it between the socket and the device, put the device into standby and read the watts.
Many devices draw power around the clock even though they seem "off" – it adds up over the year.
A switchable power strip fully disconnects several devices at once.
Connecting a smart meter directly is planned for a future version.

summary.good: Very efficient – barely any standby use.
summary.medium: Moderate standby use – savings potential here.
summary.high: High standby use – hidden power drains at work.

room_temperature.title: Room Climate Check
room_temperature.short: Measure room temperature and find your heating savings potential.

intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person measuring the room climate with a thermometer
steps: Place a thermometer in the room
details: Place the thermometer in the middle of the room at about sitting height – not right next to a radiator, window or external wall.
Wait a few minutes for the reading to stabilise before noting it.
As a guide: about 20 °C in the living room, 18 °C in the kitchen and 16–18 °C in the bedroom.
A comfortable humidity is around 40–60 %.
Every degree less saves noticeable heating energy – without becoming uncomfortable.

status.tooCold: A bit cool – every degree less saves, but mind comfort.
status.tooWarm: A bit warm – 1 °C less noticeably cuts heating costs.
status.tooDry: Rather dry – airing out or plants can help.
status.tooHumid: Rather humid – regular airing prevents mold.

draftStatus.strong: Strong draft – checking the seals pays off.

saving.title: Potential saving
saving.context: {{percent}}% less heating energy at {{delta}} °C above 20 °C – proportional for this room. Rule of thumb (≈6% per °C).

freezer.title: Freezer Check
freezer.short: Check ice build-up and temperature to avoid wasted electricity.

intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person checking the iced-up freezer
details: Estimate the ice layer on the inner wall: from about 5 mm onwards, electricity use rises noticeably.
For the temperature, place the thermometer in the middle between the frozen items and close the door again.
Recommended is around −18 °C – colder brings hardly any benefit but costs more electricity.
Defrosting regularly saves energy and frees up space again.
Check the door seal – if it no longer closes tightly, the appliance ices up faster.

run.noFrostHint: Great – ice-free means it runs efficiently, nothing to do.
run.severityLabel: How heavy is the ice? (for the estimate)
run.energyHint: Run an energy-cost meter for a few hours before defrosting (read kWh + hours), defrost, then measure again.

method.estimate: Estimated from the ice build-up level.

summary.high: Heavy frost – defrosting now saves noticeable power.

tempStatus.tooCold: too cold – setting warmer saves power

impact.title: Your savings potential
impact.info: Rough estimate: the euro savings are converted to kilowatt-hours via your electricity price and projected at about 0.38 kg CO₂ per kWh.
impact.empty: Take your first measurement to see your potential.

roomPicker.subtitle: Which room do you want to measure?

flow.planSubtitle: Recommended measurements in a sensible order.

hot_water_wait.title: Hot water wait
hot_water_wait.short: Measure how long you wait for hot water — and the potential that runs off unused.

intro.lead: Until hot water arrives, cold water often runs off unused. Find out how much.
intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person timing how long until hot water arrives
steps: Stop once it is clearly warm
details: Until hot water arrives, cold water often runs off unused – this check measures that wait.
Turn the tap fully to hot and start the stopwatch at the same moment.
Stop as soon as the water feels clearly warm.
Measure at the tap you use most often (e.g. shower or kitchen).
Long waits point to long pipe runs – a circulation line or pipe insulation can help.

run.instruction: Turn the water fully to hot, start the stopwatch — and stop as soon as it gets clearly warm.

result.explanation: It took {{seconds}} sec for hot water to arrive. In that time about {{liters}} L of drinking water ran off unused — it could be collected and reused.

furniture_spacing.title: Furniture spacing check
furniture_spacing.short: Check whether furniture or rugs are blocking your heating.

intro.lead: Unobstructed heating surfaces release much more heat. Check room by room whether anything blocks it.
intro.imageAlt: Illustration: measuring the distance between a radiator and a sofa
details: Unobstructed heating surfaces release noticeably more heat into the room – blocked ones lose part of it.
Aim to keep roughly 10 cm of clearance in front of radiators so the heat can circulate freely.
Sofas, cabinets, long curtains or covers placed right in front slow down heat output.
With underfloor heating, large flat furniture without legs and thick rugs hinder the output.
Check each room individually – often a small rearrangement already improves heating performance.

radiator.q1: Is a large piece of furniture (sofa, cabinet, shelf) right in front of the radiator?
radiator.q2: Is the radiator covered by a cover, curtain or shelf?

underfloor.q1: Are large flat furniture pieces without legs sitting directly on the floor (sofa, bed, cabinet)?
underfloor.q2: Is a large, thick rug lying on the heated area?

summary.good: Your heating surface is clear – it releases its full heat into the room.
summary.medium: Almost all clear – one small thing could still be improved.
summary.elevated: Parts of the heating surface are blocked – noticeable heat is lost here.
summary.high: The heating surface is heavily blocked – much of the heat never reaches the room.

radiator: Keep the radiator clear – leave some space around sofas/cabinets.
Avoid covers or long curtains in front of it.
Let room air flow freely around the thermostat valve.
underfloor: Choose furniture with legs – an air gap to the floor helps.
Avoid large, thick rugs on the heated area.
Prefer thin rugs suitable for underfloor heating.

base_load.title: Base-load check
base_load.short: Measure how much power your home draws around the clock.

intro.lead: Even with nothing actively running, your home draws power continuously – standby, fridges, router & co. This check reveals that base load.
intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person reading the base load on the electricity meter
steps: Switch off active devices – leave always-on ones (fridge, router) running
Read the power or measure briefly
intro.hint: Tip: it is most accurate at night or when nobody is actively using power.
details: Even with nothing actively running, your home draws power continuously – standby, fridges, router & co.
For the measurement, switch off all actively used devices but leave always-on ones like the fridge and router running.
Then read the power at the meter (digital meters show watts directly).
It is most accurate at night or when nobody is actively using power.
Even 20–30 watts of constant load add up to noticeable costs over the year.

modeHint.instant: Modern meters show the current power directly in watts – just read it off.
modeHint.timed: Read the meter, start the stopwatch, wait a few minutes, read again.
modeHint.ferraris: Older meters with a spinning disc: count the revolutions during the measured time.

watchHint.timed: Start, wait a few minutes, then stop.

summary.good: Your base load is low – barely any unnecessary continuous draw here.
summary.medium: Solid base load with a little room to improve.
summary.elevated: Your base load is elevated – worth checking the always-on consumers.
summary.high: High base load – a lot of power is drawn continuously. You should track down the causes.

result.note: Orientation values – base load depends on household size and appliances. This check does not count towards savings itself; the follow-up checks quantify the actual savings.
result.funnelText: Use the standby check to track down the biggest continuous consumers and see what can be switched off.

lighting.title: Lighting check
lighting.short: Find out how much switching to LED saves.

intro.lead: Old incandescent and halogen bulbs use many times more than LEDs. Check room by room what is worth it.
intro.imageAlt: Illustration: person showing smart lighting via app
details: Old incandescent and halogen bulbs use many times more energy than LEDs – at the same brightness.
Per room, count the bulbs that are not yet LED (incandescent, halogen or old energy-saving lamps).
LEDs come in every socket (E27, E14, GU10 …) and light colour – from warm white to daylight.
The switch pays off most where lights burn longest (living room, kitchen, hallway).
LEDs last far longer – saving electricity and frequent bulb changes.

run.bulbsTitle: Bulbs that are not LED yet
run.bulbsHint: For each type, count the bulbs in this room that have not been switched to LED yet.
run.hoursHint: How long is the light on here on average?

summary.good: Everything is already LED here – great, hardly anything more to gain.
summary.medium: A few bulbs could still be switched to LED cheaply.
summary.elevated: Switching to LED saves noticeable power here.
summary.high: Lots to gain here – the lighting uses unnecessarily much.

tip.good: Keep choosing warm-white LEDs when you replace bulbs.
tip.medium: Swap bulbs for LED step by step – start with the ones that are on the longest.
tip.elevated: Switching to LED clearly pays off. Mind the right socket (e.g. GU10, E27) and light colour.
tip.high: Convert these first: switching to LED usually pays for itself within a few months.

completed.title: Setup complete
completed.subtitle: Your household profile has been saved.

step0.subtitle: How thorough do you want to start? More detail means more accurate recommendations.
step0.note: No pressure – you can switch modes and add details later any time.

step1.statusHint: Affects which measures are realistic for you.

step4.hotWaterHint: Hot water is often a major cost factor.

hub.title: Profile overview
hub.subtitle: Pick a section to complete or change your details.

prices.title: Energy prices
prices.subtitle: Optional: enter your real prices – then we calculate with your tariffs instead of averages. Leaving fields empty is perfectly fine.
prices.laterNote: You can change these values anytime later in Monitoring.

info.electricity: On your electricity bill or supply contract – the unit price in ct/kWh.
info.water: On your water/utility statement – price per cubic metre (€/m³).
info.gas: On your gas bill or annual statement – here as price per cubic metre (€/m³).
info.oil: On your delivery/tank invoice – price per litre (€/l).
info.pellets: On your delivery/annual statement – price per kilogram (€/kg).
info.heat_pump: If your heat pump has its own electricity tariff – otherwise like household power (ct/kWh).

tariff.title: Electricity price

prompt.title: Do you know your electricity price?
prompt.text: With your electricity price the app can calculate real euro amounts instead of just kWh.

tariff.workPriceInfo: Price per kilowatt-hour of electricity consumed.
tariff.basePriceInfo: Fixed monthly base fee independent of consumption.
tariff.cardInfo: The electricity price is used to convert your consumption into real euro amounts.

whereToFind.title: Where do I find my electricity price?
whereToFind.text: On your last electricity bill or in your supply contract. The unit price is given in ct/kWh, the base price in €/month.

tariff.skipHint: We'll then calculate with average values.
tariff.soonText: More meters (water, gas, pellets) are coming.

overview.title: Monitoring
overview.subtitle: All your meters at a glance.
overview.trackHint: Log readings regularly to see your trend.
overview.ghostHint: This is how your trend will look once you enter two meter readings.

readings.title: Electricity meter
readings.emptyText: Enter your current meter reading to start tracking.
readings.needTwo: At least 2 readings are needed to see your consumption.

price.intro: With the price, the app converts your consumption into real euro amounts.
price.label: Price
price.workInfo: Price per consumed unit (e.g. kWh, m³, litre).
price.whereTitle: Where do I find the price?
price.whereText: On your latest bill or in your supplier contract.

onboarding.title: Setup
onboarding.subtitle: A few questions about your home – so the app can guide you properly.
points: Rooms, living area and year the building was built
Heating type (heat pump, gas, oil, pellets, wood …)
Hot water: central, decentral or via the heating system
Number of residents
Available measuring devices (thermometer, power meter …)

points: Guided flow: explanation → measurement → result
Examples like the shower head test or fridge check
Instantly understandable tips for each result

monitoring.title: Monitoring

points: Enter meter readings for electricity, water, gas, pellets
Consumption shown as a clear chart
Reminders for regular readings

university.title: HTW Berlin · GEIT
university.subtitle: Lab experiments in the Building Energy and Information Technology programme.

quiz.question: Question
quiz.feedbackPassed: Well prepared – good luck in the lab!
quiz.feedbackFailed: Review the preparation once more and try again.

profiles.hint: Tap a home to switch, or add a new one.
profiles.deleteConfirm: Permanently delete this home? All measurements, meter readings and resident access will be lost for good.

share.title: Share home
share.explainer: Send this link to your housemates. Anyone who opens it and signs in will see and edit this home together with you.
share.inviteMessage:
🏠 E-App invitation
I'm sharing my home profile “{{profile}}” with you – so we can track meter readings, consumption and energy costs together.
Just tap the link to join (free, takes a minute):
share.rotateHint: "Generate new link" immediately invalidates the old link – housemates who already joined will stay.
share.transferConfirm: Make “{{name}}” the owner? You will become an editor and can no longer manage or delete this home.
share.memberLimit: Member limit reached ({{max}}). An upgrade is required to add more residents.
share.error: That didn't work, unfortunately. Please try again later.

join.title: Invitation to a home
join.loginPrompt: You've been invited to a shared home. Sign in (or register for free) to join.
join.successMessage: You now see the same measurements, meter readings and reports as your housemates.
join.invalidMessage: This invitation link is invalid or has been revoked. Please ask for a new link.

hero.title: Estimated energy costs
hero.label: Estimated energy costs / year
hero.info: This is a rough estimate based on your profile (people, living area) and the stored electricity price. With real measurements and meter readings the figure becomes much more accurate.

tips: Lower the room temperature by 1 °C – it saves about 6 % of heating energy.
Standby devices quietly draw power: switchable power strips help.
Air the room briefly with wide-open windows instead of tilting them to save heat and prevent mould.
When buying new appliances, choose the highest energy efficiency class.
Washing laundry at 30 °C instead of 60 °C saves up to two thirds of the electricity.
Set the fridge to 7 °C – any colder just wastes energy.

data.title: Reset data
data.subtitle: Reset individual areas or all of your data. This cannot be undone.
data.confirmQuestion: Really reset? This cannot be undone.

profile.title: Profile / inputs
profile.desc: Onboarding details: rooms, building, heating, etc.

all.title: Reset everything
all.desc: Deletes all user data (profile, measurements, tariffs, meter readings, learning progress). Theme and language are kept; the introduction is shown again.

demo.title: View the example home
demo.body: We'll load a fully set-up sample home with lots of measurements and regular meter readings. Your current view will be replaced. Signed in? Your real home stays untouched and comes back once you leave the demo.

slides.title: How much really flows through?
slides.body: The showerhead test works out litres per minute in 30 seconds – and what an eco showerhead saves.

slides.title: Your consumption becomes visible
slides.body: Enter meter readings – just two readings reveal your consumption and trend.

slides.title: See your savings potential
slides.body: Your profile and real measurements turn into your personal savings target in euros.

tips.title: Recommendations
tips.subtitle: Concrete measures for your home – sorted by impact.
tips.empty: Run a few measurements first – matching tips will appear here.

standby.title: Cut standby
standby.reason: Biggest standby item: your $t(measurements.standby.deviceTypes.{{deviceType}}) at {{watts}} W. A switchable power strip kills the constant draw.

showerhead.title: Save water in the shower
showerhead.reason: Your showerhead delivers {{flow}} L/min. A water-saving head (~8 L/min) cuts hot water noticeably – with full comfort.

lighting.title: Switch to LED
lighting.reason: {{count}} bulbs still aren't LED. Switching pays off fast, especially where lights burn for hours.

hot_water_wait.title: Reduce hot-water wait
hot_water_wait.reason: About {{seconds}} s until warm water arrives – it runs off unused all that time. Insulated pipes or shorter runs help.

fridge.title: Set the fridge warmer
fridge.reason: {{temp}} °C is colder than needed. 7 °C is plenty – every degree warmer saves around 6 % power, safely.

fridge_warm.title: Set the fridge colder
fridge_warm.reason: {{temp}} °C is too warm for safe storage. Set it to 5–7 °C – cold enough without needless icing.

freezer.title: Defrost the freezer
freezer.reason: A layer of ice drives up consumption. Defrosting costs nothing and saves noticeable power.

room_temperature.title: Lower room temperature
room_temperature.reason: Just 1 °C less saves around 6 % heating energy – usually without any real loss of comfort.

room_cold.title: Don't let rooms cool down
room_cold.reason: Persistently cold, damp rooms invite mould. Steady, moderate heating is healthier and often more efficient than constant reheating.

humidity_high.title: Ventilate against damp
humidity_high.reason: Humidity is too high – short bursts of ventilation several times a day prevent mould. A hygrometer keeps it in view.

humidity_low.title: Air is too dry
humidity_low.reason: Very dry air irritates airways. Don't overheat and ventilate briefly – a hygrometer shows when it's right.

draft.title: Seal draughts
draft.reason: Noticeable draughts at windows or doors waste heat. Self-adhesive sealing strips close the gaps in minutes.

furniture_spacing.title: Clear the radiator
furniture_spacing.reason: Unobstructed heating surfaces release more heat. Reflective foil behind them puts extra degrees into the room, not the wall.

base_load.title: Track down always-on consumers
base_load.reason: Your base load is {{watts}} W – even with nothing actively on. A plug-in meter helps you find the hidden always-on consumers.

smart_thermostat.title: Smart radiator thermostats
smart_thermostat.reason: Automatic setback and schedules save heating energy without you having to think about it.

owner_control.title: Add heating control
owner_control.reason: As an owner, central smart control pays off – schedules and setback for the whole home.

tips.allHandled: All handled – nice! New measurements will surface fresh recommendations here.

scan.title: Scan meter reading
scan.hint: Only the digit row in the frame – get close, good light
scan.confirmHint: Is the number correct? Adjust if needed.
scan.noDigits: No digits found – type the number or scan again
scan.errorPermission: No camera access. Allow the camera in your browser settings and try again.
scan.errorInsecure: The camera needs a secure connection (HTTPS).
